title: Sleep Disorders
authors: Thomas E. Scammell, Clifford B. Saper, Charles A. Czeisler

items.subtopic: Sleep Disorders | PHYSIOLOGY OF SLEEP AND WAKEFULNESS
items.title: Prevalence and Economic Impact of Sleep Disorders in Adults
items.content: More than half of adults in the United States experience intermittent sleep disturbances, with only 30% consistently obtaining sufficient sleep. The National Academy of Medicine estimates that 50-70 million Americans suffer from chronic disorders of sleep and wakefulness, affecting daytime functioning and health.
keyPoints: Over 50% of U.S. adults report sleep disturbance intermittently.
Only 30% obtain sufficient sleep consistently.
50-70 million Americans have chronic sleep disorders.
>80% of patients with sleep disorders remain undiagnosed and untreated.
Undiagnosed sleep disorders cost the U.S. economy >$400 billion annually.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · Sleep Disorders | PHYSIOLOGY OF SLEEP AND WAKEFULNESS

items.subtopic: Sleep Disorders | PHYSIOLOGY OF SLEEP AND WAKEFULNESS
items.title: Stages of Sleep Defined by Polysomnography
items.content: Polysomnography uses EEG, EOG, and EMG to define sleep stages: REM and NREM (N1, N2, N3). REM is characterized by low-amplitude EEG, bursts of eye movements, and muscle paralysis except in respiratory muscles. NREM stages show increasing thresholds for arousal and slowing cortical EEG.
keyPoints: Polysomnography records EEG, EOG, and EMG to define sleep stages.
Two basic states: REM and NREM (N1, N2, N3).
REM sleep has low-amplitude mixed-frequency EEG and bursts of eye movements.
Muscle paralysis occurs in REM except for respiratory muscles.
NREM stages show increasing thresholds for arousal and slowing cortical EEG.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · Sleep Disorders | PHYSIOLOGY OF SLEEP AND WAKEFULNESS

items.title: Impact of Sleep Deprivation on REM and NREM Sleep Recovery
items.content: Sleep deprivation affects the recovery pattern of sleep stages, with NREM sleep recovering before REM sleep. This sequence is crucial for understanding how to manage sleep disorders and optimize diagnostic procedures like polysomnography.
keyPoints: NREM sleep generally recovers first after sleep deprivation.
REM sleep follows in recovery but may be selectively deprived if sleep is truncated (e.g., by an alarm clock).
To ensure accurate diagnostic results, patients should have at least 8 hours of sleep for several nights before a polysomnogram.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · ORGANIZATION OF HUMAN SLEEP

items.title: Age-related Changes in Sleep Architecture and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: Sleep architecture changes significantly with age, affecting the distribution of NREM and REM sleep. Understanding these changes is vital for diagnosing and managing sleep disorders across different age groups.
keyPoints: N3 sleep is most intense during childhood but decreases with age, often disappearing in older adults.
Older adults experience more fragmented NREM sleep and frequent awakenings.
In young adults, REM sleep constitutes 20-25% of total sleep time, while NREM stages N1 and N2 make up 50-60%.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · ORGANIZATION OF HUMAN SLEEP

items.subtopic: WAKE AND SLEEP ARE REGULATED BY BRAIN CIRCUITS
items.title: Mechanisms of Arousal and Sleep Regulation
items.content: The expression of wakefulness and sleep is controlled by two principal neural systems: the ascending arousal system, which activates various brain regions to promote wakefulness using neurotransmitters like monoamines, glutamate, acetylcholine, and GABA; and the sleep-promoting system, which inhibits arousal through GABAergic neurons in specific brain areas.
keyPoints: Ascending arousal system involves nerve cells from upper pons to hypothalamus and basal forebrain activating cerebral cortex and thalamus.
Neurotransmitters used include norepinephrine, dopamine, serotonin, histamine, glutamate, acetylcholine, and GABA.
Orexin (hypocretin) neurons in the hypothalamus promote arousal.
Damage to rostral pons/midbrain causes coma; damage to midbrain/hypothalamus causes profound sleepiness.
Loss of orexin neurons leads to narcolepsy.
Thalamic damage results in a persistent vegetative state with preserved wake-sleep cycles.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · WAKE AND SLEEP ARE REGULATED BY BRAIN CIRCUITS

items.subtopic: WAKE AND SLEEP ARE REGULATED BY BRAIN CIRCUITS
items.title: Mutual Inhibition Between Arousal and Sleep Systems
items.content: The transition between sleep and wake states is facilitated by a mutual inhibition mechanism between the arousal and sleep-promoting systems, functioning like a 'flip-flop switch' to ensure rapid transitions without intermediate states.
keyPoints: Arousal system turned off during sleep via inhibitory inputs from GABAergic neurons in preoptic area and pons.
Lateral hypothalamus promotes REM sleep with melanin-concentrating hormone.
Mutual inhibition between arousal and sleep systems enables rapid transitions, consistent with EEG observations.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · WAKE AND SLEEP ARE REGULATED BY BRAIN CIRCUITS

items.subtopic: WAKE AND SLEEP ARE REGULATED BY BRAIN CIRCUITS
items.title: Regulation of REM Sleep and Associated Disorders
items.content: REM sleep is regulated by a flip-flop switch mechanism involving GABAergic REM-Off neurons inhibiting REM-On neurons, with cholinergic input promoting REM transitions. Disruption in this system can lead to disorders like REM sleep behavior disorder.
keyPoints: GABAergic REM-Off neurons inhibit REM-On neurons in the upper pons.
REM-On neurons project to medulla and spinal cord to induce muscle atonia during REM sleep.
Cholinergic input promotes REM transitions; monoaminergic input inhibits them.
Drugs increasing monoamine tone reduce REM sleep.
Damage to REM sleep paralysis mechanism causes REM sleep behavior disorder.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · WAKE AND SLEEP ARE REGULATED BY BRAIN CIRCUITS

items.subtopic: SLEEP-WAKE CYCLES ARE DRIVEN BY HOMEOSTATIC, ALLOSTATIC, AND CIRCADIAN INPUTS
items.title: Homeostatic Mechanisms in Sleep Regulation
items.content: The homeostatic mechanism regulating sleep is influenced by prolonged wakefulness, which increases adenosine levels in the brain. Adenosine acts through A1 receptors to inhibit arousal-promoting regions and promotes sleep via A2a receptors, which are blocked by caffeine.
keyPoints: Prolonged wakefulness leads to increased adenosine levels.
Adenosine inhibits arousal through A1 receptors.
Sleep promotion occurs via A2a receptor activation.
Caffeine blocks A2a receptors to counteract sleepiness.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · SLEEP-WAKE CYCLES ARE DRIVEN BY HOMEOSTATIC, ALLOSTATIC, AND CIRCADIAN INPUTS

items.subtopic: SLEEP-WAKE CYCLES ARE DRIVEN BY HOMEOSTATIC, ALLOSTATIC, AND CIRCADIAN INPUTS
items.title: Impact of Allostasis and Circadian Rhythms on Sleep
items.content: Allostasis affects sleep through stress responses that can lead to insomnia, particularly in anxiety disorders. The circadian timing signal from the suprachiasmatic nuclei (SCN) regulates wakefulness and sleep by imposing 24-hour rhythms.
keyPoints: Stress-induced insomnia is common due to allostasis.
PET studies show hyperactivation of arousal systems in chronic insomnia.
Circadian regulation involves SCN outputs to the hypothalamus.
Behavioral approaches like CBT are effective for long-term treatment.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · SLEEP-WAKE CYCLES ARE DRIVEN BY HOMEOSTATIC, ALLOSTATIC, AND CIRCADIAN INPUTS

items.title: Understanding Circadian Rhythms and Their Impact on Sleep
items.content: Circadian rhythms, driven by endogenous oscillatory processes, regulate various physiological functions including sleep. The SCN maintains these rhythms even in isolation, synchronizing with the external light-dark cycle via retinal input.
keyPoints: Endogenous circadian rhythmicity is regulated by molecular genetic feedback loops (~24 h duration).
SCN neurons maintain a near-24-h synchronous rhythm of neural activity and synchronize with environmental light-dark cycles through retinal input.
Humans are sensitive to blue light (460–500 nm) for resetting circadian rhythms, influencing sleep patterns.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PHYSIOLOGY OF CIRCADIAN RHYTHMICITY (part 1)

items.title: Circadian Misalignment and Its Consequences
items.content: Misalignment between the endogenous circadian pacemaker and desired wake-sleep cycles can lead to insomnia, decreased alertness, and impaired performance, affecting night-shift workers and travelers.
keyPoints: Endogenous circadian rhythm for wake propensity peaks before bedtime; sleep propensity peaks near wake time.
Misalignment with the wake-sleep cycle can cause insomnia and impair performance.
Sleep regularity is crucial for physical and mental health outcomes.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PHYSIOLOGY OF CIRCADIAN RHYTHMICITY (part 1)

items.title: Dream Recall and Sleep Stages
items.content: REM sleep is associated with vivid dream recall, occurring more than 80% of the time when awakened from this stage.
keyPoints: Participants report vivid dream imagery >80% of the time when awakened from REM sleep.
Vivid imagery may also occur after NREM sleep interruptions.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PHYSIOLOGY OF CIRCADIAN RHYTHMICITY (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Sleep on Neurotransmission and Amyloid Clearance
items.content: During sleep, changes in intracellular volume lead to increased extracellular space in the brain. This facilitates hyperpolarization and reduced neuronal firing due to higher calcium and lower potassium concentrations. The expanded extracellular space enhances the diffusion and clearance of substances like $\beta$-amyloid peptide via cerebrospinal fluid flow.
keyPoints: Sleep increases extracellular space in the brain, supporting hyperpolarization and reduced neuronal firing.
Higher calcium and lower potassium concentrations during sleep enhance $\beta$-amyloid clearance.
Inadequate sleep may contribute to the accumulation of $\beta$-amyloid peptide, a key factor in Alzheimer's disease pathogenesis.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PHYSIOLOGY OF CIRCADIAN RHYTHMICITY (part 2)

items.title: Role of Melatonin in Sleep Efficiency and Disorders
items.content: Melatonin secretion, controlled by the SCN, is inhibited by ambient light. It peaks at night and enhances sleep efficiency when aligned with endogenous levels. Exogenous melatonin can aid sleep onset in conditions like delayed sleep-wake phase disorder or sympathetic denervation of the pineal gland but is often ineffective for primary insomnia.
keyPoints: Melatonin secretion is highest at night, controlled by SCN, and inhibited by light.
Exogenous melatonin aids sleep onset when endogenous levels are low (e.g., DSWPD).
Melatonin administration may help patients with sympathetic denervation of the pineal gland (e.g., cervical spinal cord injury or Parkinson's disease) at 3 mg 30 min before bedtime.
Melatonin is often ineffective for primary insomnia due to high endogenous levels.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PHYSIOLOGY OF CIRCADIAN RHYTHMICITY (part 2)

items.title: Importance of Comprehensive History in Sleep Disorder Diagnosis
items.content: A detailed patient history is crucial for diagnosing sleep disorders. This includes understanding the duration, severity, and consistency of symptoms, as well as their impact on daily functioning. Information from bed partners or family members can be invaluable, especially when patients are unaware of certain symptoms like heavy snoring or unintentional napping during inappropriate times.
keyPoints: Duration, severity, and consistency of symptoms are important for diagnosis.
Patient's estimate of consequences on waking function is crucial.
Information from bed partners can reveal unnoticed symptoms such as heavy snoring or falling asleep at work.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · Sleep Disorders

items.title: Role of Polysomnography and MSLT in Diagnosing Sleep Disorders
items.content: Polysomnography is essential for diagnosing disorders like sleep apnea, narcolepsy, and periodic limb movement disorder. It measures various parameters including sleep stages and respiratory effort. The multiple sleep latency test (MSLT) assesses daytime sleep propensity, aiding in the diagnosis of narcolepsy and other causes of excessive sleepiness.
keyPoints: Polysomnography is necessary for diagnosing sleep apnea, narcolepsy, and PLMD.
A conventional polysomnogram measures sleep stages, respiratory effort, oxygen saturation, limb movements, and heart rhythm.
The MSLT measures daytime sleep propensity and helps diagnose narcolepsy.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · Sleep Disorders

items.title: Impact of Daytime Sleepiness on Functionality and Safety
items.content: Daytime sleepiness significantly impairs an individual's ability to perform optimally in various settings, including school, work, and driving. It is associated with a high risk of motor vehicle crashes due to drowsy drivers.
keyPoints: Up to 25% of adults experience persistent daytime sleepiness that affects performance.
More than half of Americans have fallen asleep while driving.
An estimated 1.2 million motor vehicle crashes per year are attributed to drowsy drivers, causing about 20% of all serious crash injuries and deaths.
Twenty-four hours of continuous wakefulness impairs reaction time as much as a blood alcohol concentration of 0.10 g/dL.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · EVALUATION OF DAYTIME SLEEPINESS

items.title: Differentiating Sleepiness from Fatigue
items.content: Sleepiness and fatigue are distinct conditions, with sleepiness being a propensity to fall asleep and fatigue being a feeling of low energy without the tendency to sleep.
keyPoints: Sleepiness is most evident when sedentary; fatigue interferes with active pursuits.
Sleepiness occurs with disorders affecting sleep quality or neural arousal mechanisms.
Fatigue is more common in inflammatory disorders, post-COVID syndrome, fibromyalgia, myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome, and endocrine deficiencies.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · EVALUATION OF DAYTIME SLEEPINESS

items.title: Objective Measurement of Daytime Sleepiness
items.content: The Multiple Sleep Latency Test (MSLT) is used to objectively measure a patient's propensity to sleep under quiet conditions, with specific diagnostic criteria.
keyPoints: An overnight polysomnogram should precede the MSLT to ensure adequate nighttime sleep.
The MSLT consists of five 20-min nap opportunities every 2 hours across the day.
Average sleep latency <8 min is considered objective evidence of excessive daytime sleepiness.
REM sleep during two or more naps supports a diagnosis of narcolepsy.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · EVALUATION OF DAYTIME SLEEPINESS

items.title: Insufficient Sleep: Prevalence and Impact on Daytime Functioning
items.content: Insufficient sleep is the most common cause of excessive daytime sleepiness. The average U.S. adult gets only 6.75 hours of sleep per weeknight, with only 30% reporting sufficient sleep. This issue is prevalent among shift workers, individuals working multiple jobs, lower socioeconomic groups, and historically minority populations. Teenagers often need at least 9 hours but fail to get enough due to social pressures and late evening activities.
keyPoints: Average adult needs 7.5–8 hours of sleep per night.
Only 30% of U.S. adults report sufficient sleep.
Teenagers typically require ≥9 hours of sleep.
Insufficient sleep can lead to inattentiveness, irritability, and depression.
A 2-week sleep log is diagnostically useful for assessing sleep patterns.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · INSUFFICIENT SLEEP | SLEEP APNEA SYNDROMES

items.title: Sleep Apnea Syndromes: Prevalence, Risks, and Importance of Diagnosis
items.content: Sleep apnea syndromes are a serious cause of excessive daytime sleepiness and disturbed nocturnal sleep. At least 24% of middle-aged men and 9% of middle-aged women experience breathing cessation during sleep. Untreated sleep apnea increases risks for motor vehicle crashes, depression, hypertension, myocardial infarction, diabetes, stroke, and mortality.
keyPoints: 24% of middle-aged men and 9% of middle-aged women have sleep apnea.
9% of men and 4% of women experience over a hundred episodes per night.
Sleep apnea is prevalent in overweight individuals and the elderly.
Undiagnosed sleep apnea increases risk for various health issues.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · INSUFFICIENT SLEEP | SLEEP APNEA SYNDROMES

items.title: Understanding Narcolepsy: Key Characteristics and Prevalence
items.content: Narcolepsy is characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, hypnagogic/hypnopompic hallucinations, and sleep paralysis. It affects about 1 in 200 people in the United States and typically begins between ages 10 and 20.
keyPoints: Narcolepsy is characterized by excessive daytime sleepiness, cataplexy, hypnagogic/hypnopompic hallucinations, and sleep paralysis.
Affects about 1 in 200 people in the United States.
Typically begins between age 10 and 20.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · NARCOLEPSY (part 1)

items.title: Pathophysiology of Narcolepsy: Role of Orexin Neurons
items.content: Narcolepsy is caused by the loss of hypothalamic neurons that produce orexin neuropeptides. Type 1 narcolepsy involves very low or undetectable levels of orexins in CSF, while type 2 narcolepsy usually has normal orexin levels.
keyPoints: Caused by loss of hypothalamic neurons producing orexin neuropeptides.
Type 1 narcolepsy: very low or undetectable orexin levels in CSF.
Type 2 narcolepsy: normal orexin levels.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · NARCOLEPSY (part 1)

items.title: Diagnosis of Narcolepsy: Key Features and Triggers
items.content: Diagnosed by chronic sleepiness plus cataplexy or other symptoms. Cataplexy, triggered by strong emotions, is diagnostically specific to narcolepsy type 1.
keyPoints: Diagnosis involves chronic sleepiness plus cataplexy or other symptoms.
Cataplexy is often triggered by strong emotions like laughter or surprise.
Occurs in about half of all narcolepsy patients, distinguishing narcolepsy type 1.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · NARCOLEPSY (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Suspected Narcolepsy
items.content: When narcolepsy is suspected, a polysomnogram followed by an MSLT the next day is essential for diagnosis. The polysomnogram rules out other causes of daytime sleepiness and confirms adequate prior night's sleep, while the MSLT provides objective evidence of excessive sleepiness and REM sleep dysregulation.
keyPoints: Polysomnogram followed by an MSLT is required to diagnose narcolepsy.
Patients with narcolepsy typically fall asleep in <8 min on average during the MSLT.
REM sleep appears within 15 min of sleep onset at night, rare in healthy individuals.
Stimulants should be stopped 1 week before the MSLT; antidepressants for 3 weeks prior.
Patients should have adequate sleep each night for a week before testing.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · NARCOLEPSY (part 2) | Narcolepsy

items.title: Treatment Strategies for Narcolepsy and Cataplexy
items.content: Symptomatic treatment of narcolepsy includes promoting adequate sleep, scheduled naps, and medications. Modafinil is preferred due to fewer side effects compared to amphetamines. Antidepressants like venlafaxine or fluoxetine are effective for cataplexy by increasing noradrenergic or serotonergic tone.
keyPoints: Narcolepsy treatment includes adequate sleep and a 15-20 min afternoon nap.
Modafinol (200–400 mg daily) is often used due to fewer side effects than amphetamines.
Methylphenidate (10–20 mg bid) and dextroamphetamine (10 mg bid) are effective but have potential for abuse.
Venlafaxine (37.5–150 mg each morning) and fluoxetine (10–40 mg each morning) improve cataplexy.
Sodium oxybate promotes continuous slow wave sleep, improving alertness and reducing cataplexy.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · NARCOLEPSY (part 2) | Narcolepsy

items.title: Understanding the Prevalence and Onset of Insomnia
items.content: Insomnia is a common sleep disorder characterized by difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, leading to dissatisfaction with sleep quality. It affects approximately 10% of adults chronically and over 30% acutely.
keyPoints: Chronic insomnia lasts >3 months and occurs in ~10% of adults.
Acute insomnia affects >30% of adults.
Insomnia is more prevalent among women, older adults, those with lower socioeconomic status, and individuals with medical, psychiatric, or substance abuse disorders.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · EVALUATION OF INSOMNIA (part 1)

items.title: Role of Polysomnogram in Insomnia Diagnosis
items.content: Polysomnograms are seldom used in evaluating insomnia as they typically confirm subjective reports without adding new insights. They show increased beta activity during sleep, which aligns with patients' feelings of wakefulness.
keyPoints: Polysomnogram confirms long latency to sleep and numerous awakenings.
Increased fast (beta) EEG activity during sleep is common in insomnia.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · EVALUATION OF INSOMNIA (part 1)

items.title: Impact of Psychiatric Conditions on Insomnia
items.content: A significant proportion of patients with psychiatric disorders experience sleep disturbances, with about half of chronic insomnia cases associated with such conditions. Depression, anxiety, and other disorders can disrupt sleep patterns.
keyPoints: 80% of patients with psychiatric disorders have sleep complaints.
About 50% of chronic insomnia is linked to a psychiatric disorder.
Depression often causes early morning awakening; anxiety leads to racing thoughts.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · EVALUATION OF INSOMNIA (part 1)

items.title: Medical Conditions and Their Impact on Sleep Quality
items.content: Various medical conditions can disrupt sleep, including rheumatologic disorders, respiratory diseases like asthma and obstructive sleep apnea, menopausal hormonal changes, and gastroesophageal reflux. Long COVID is also associated with insomnia in approximately 60% of patients.
keyPoints: Pain from rheumatologic disorders or neuropathy disrupts sleep.

Respiratory conditions such as asthma, COPD, cystic fibrosis, congestive heart failure, and restrictive lung disease can worsen at night due to circadian variations in airway resistance and postural changes causing nocturnal dyspnea.
Obstructive sleep apnea is exacerbated by loss of airway muscle tone during sleep.
Menopausal hormonal changes often lead to poor sleep in women.
Gastroesophageal reflux commonly causes difficulty sleeping.
Approximately 60% of patients with long COVID report symptoms of insomnia.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · EVALUATION OF INSOMNIA (part 2) | Insomnia

items.title: Neurologic Disorders and Their Association with Sleep Disturbances
items.content: Dementia, Parkinson's disease, and rare conditions like fatal familial insomnia are linked to poor sleep. In dementia, factors include altered circadian rhythms and weakened brain sleep-promoting mechanisms, leading to nighttime wandering and caregiver burden.
keyPoints: Dementia is associated with poor sleep due to napping during the day, altered circadian rhythms, and weakened sleep-promoting mechanisms.
Insomnia and nighttime wandering in dementia can lead to institutionalization due to caregiver burden.
Cognitively intact elderly men with fragmented sleep may experience subsequent cognitive decline.
Parkinson's disease patients may have poor sleep due to rigidity, dementia, urinary frequency, REM sleep behavior disorder, restless legs syndrome, among other factors.
Fatal familial insomnia is a rare neurodegenerative condition caused by mutations in the prion protein gene; it presents with insomnia, dementia, myoclonus, dysarthria, or autonomic dysfunction.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · EVALUATION OF INSOMNIA (part 2) | Insomnia

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF MEDICAL AND PSYCHIATRIC DISEASE | IMPROVE SLEEP HYGIENE | COGNITIVE BEHAVIORAL THERAPY
items.title: Addressing Underlying Medical or Psychiatric Conditions to Treat Insomnia
items.content: If a patient's insomnia is linked to an underlying medical or psychiatric condition, such as pain or depression, it should be treated directly. This may involve managing the primary condition effectively and adjusting medication timing.
keyPoints: Treat contributing conditions like pain or depression.
Improve breathing issues if present.
Adjust timing of medications to avoid interference with sleep.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · TREATMENT OF MEDICAL AND PSYCHIATRIC DISEASE | IMPROVE SLEEP HYGIENE | COGNITIVE BEHAVIORAL THERAPY

items.subtopic: TREATMENT OF MEDICAL AND PSYCHIATRIC DISEASE | IMPROVE SLEEP HYGIENE | COGNITIVE BEHAVIORAL THERAPY
items.title: Improving Sleep Hygiene to Alleviate Insomnia
items.content: Enhancing sleep hygiene involves establishing a consistent sleep schedule, creating a relaxing pre-sleep routine, and minimizing exposure to stimulating activities and devices before bedtime. Patients should aim to fall asleep within 20 minutes of getting into bed; if not, they should engage in calming activities until drowsiness returns.
keyPoints: Establish regular bedtime and wake time.
Allocate sleep duration based on actual sleep needs.
Engage in a relaxing routine before bed (e.g., warm bath, meditation).
Avoid electronics in the bedroom; use dim light if necessary.
If unable to fall asleep within 20 minutes, leave bed for calming activities.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · TREATMENT OF MEDICAL AND PSYCHIATRIC DISEASE | IMPROVE SLEEP HYGIENE | COGNITIVE BEHAVIORAL THERAPY

items.title: Choosing Benzodiazepine Receptor Agonists (BzRAs) Based on Duration of Action
items.content: Benzodiazepine receptor agonists are effective for treating insomnia and should be selected based on the desired duration of action to minimize side effects. Short-acting agents like zaleplon (half-life: 1–2 h) and triazolam/zolpidem (half-lives: 2–4 h) are suitable for those who need minimal morning sedation, while longer-acting agents like eszopiclone (5–8 h) and temazepam (8–20 h) may be used when prolonged sleep is necessary.
keyPoints: BzRAs potentiate the postsynaptic response to GABA at GABA_A receptors.
Commonly prescribed BzRAs: zaleplon (5–20 mg), zolpidem/triazolam (0.125–0.25 mg/5–10 mg), eszopiclone (1–3 mg), temazepam (15–30 mg).
Select based on half-life to minimize morning sedation and side effects.
Intermittent use recommended for chronic insomnia unless risks of untreated insomnia are high.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · MEDICATIONS FOR INSOMNIA

items.title: Considerations in Prescribing Sedatives for Insomnia
items.content: When prescribing sedatives for insomnia, consider the increased risk of falls and confusion in elderly patients. Use the lowest effective dose to minimize morning sedation, which can impair driving and judgment. Be cautious with benzodiazepines due to their potential for addiction and abuse, especially in those with a history of substance abuse. Additionally, be aware that sedatives may worsen depression and sleep apnea.
keyPoints: Sedatives increase risk of falls and confusion in the elderly; use lowest effective dose.
Morning sedation can impair driving/judgment; consider duration of action.
Benzodiazepines have addiction/abuse potential, especially with history of substance abuse.
Sedatives may worsen depression and sleep apnea.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · MEDICATIONS FOR INSOMNIA

items.title: Iron Deficiency and RLS: When to Consider Iron Replacement
items.content: Restless legs syndrome (RLS) often presents with an irresistible urge to move the legs, accompanied by uncomfortable sensations. A common treatable cause of RLS is iron deficiency. It's important to consider iron replacement therapy if a patient's ferritin level is below 75 ng/mL.
keyPoints: Iron deficiency is the most common treatable cause of RLS.
Consider iron replacement if ferritin < 75 ng/mL.
RLS affects 5–10% of adults, more common in women and older adults.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.title: Managing RLS with Medications: Alpha-2-delta Calcium Channel Ligands and Dopamine Agonists
items.content: For symptomatic treatment of restless legs syndrome (RLS), alpha-2-delta calcium channel ligands or dopamine agonists are commonly used. These medications can be particularly effective, but they come with potential side effects. Alpha-2-delta ligands like gabapentin and pregabalin provide sedation and analgesia, beneficial for patients with pain or neuropathy. Dopamine agonists such as pramipexole and ropinirole are effective but may cause augmentation in about 25% of patients.
keyPoints: Alpha-2-delta calcium channel ligands: gabapentin (300–900 mg q7PM), gabapentin enacarbil (300–600 mg q5PM), pregabalin (150–450 mg q7PM).
Dopamine agonists: pramipexole (0.25–0.5 mg q7PM), ropinirole (0.5–4 mg q7PM).
About 25% of patients on dopamine agonists may develop augmentation.
Side effects of dopamine agonists include nausea, morning sedation, and increased rewarding behaviors.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME

items.title: Characteristics of Periodic Limb Movement Disorder (PLMD)
items.content: Periodic Limb Movement Disorder is identified by rhythmic leg twitches during NREM sleep, disrupting rest. These movements are characterized by a triple flexion reflex with specific muscle actions and occur every 20–40 seconds.
keyPoints: Movements include triple flexion reflex: extensions of the great toe and dorsiflexion of the foot.
Duration of each movement is 0.5–5.0 seconds.
Frequency of movements is every 20–40 seconds during NREM sleep.
Polysomnogram with anterior tibialis recordings is used for diagnosis.
EEG shows brief arousals due to movements, leading to insomnia and daytime sleepiness.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PERIODIC LIMB MOVEMENT DISORDER

items.title: Management of Periodic Limb Movement Disorder (PLMD)
items.content: The management of PLMD often involves the use of medications effective for Restless Legs Syndrome, as both conditions share a common pathophysiology and genetic factors.
keyPoints: Dopamine agonists are commonly used to treat PLMD.
Genetic studies show polymorphisms associated with both RLS and PLMD, indicating shared pathophysiology.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PERIODIC LIMB MOVEMENT DISORDER

items.title: Understanding Sleepwalking: Causes and Management
items.content: Sleepwalking, or somnambulism, involves complex motor activities during NREM stage N3 sleep. It is most prevalent in children and adolescents and can be exacerbated by stress, alcohol, and insufficient sleep.
keyPoints: Occurs during NREM stage N3 sleep, typically within the first few hours of the night.
About 15% of children experience occasional sleepwalking; it persists in about 1% of adults.
Recurrent episodes occur in 1–6% of patients.
Familial basis present in roughly one-third of cases.
Management includes addressing stress, alcohol use, and insufficient sleep; improving home safety is crucial.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PARASOMNIAS (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Management of Sleep Terrors
items.content: Sleep terrors primarily affect young children during NREM stage N3 sleep, characterized by screaming, autonomic arousal, and difficulty in arousing the child. The condition is typically self-limited.
keyPoints: Occurs during NREM stage N3 sleep, usually within the first few hours of sleep.
Symptoms include screaming, sweating, tachycardia, large pupils, and hyperventilation.
Difficult to arouse; rarely recalled by the child in the morning.
Reassurance to parents is key as it is self-limited and benign.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PARASOMNIAS (part 1)

items.title: REM Sleep Behavior Disorder: Identification and Risks
items.content: RBD occurs during REM sleep, characterized by the absence of muscle atonia leading to violent movements. Patients often recall dreams that match these behaviors.
keyPoints: Occurs during REM sleep when muscle paralysis is typically present.
Patients exhibit agitated or violent behavior and may report matching dreams upon awakening.
Dramatic limb movements such as punching or kicking can lead to injury.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PARASOMNIAS (part 1)

items.title: Red Flags and Management of REM Sleep Behavior Disorder (RBD)
items.content: REM sleep behavior disorder is a significant predictor for developing synucleinopathies. The prevalence increases with age, affecting about 2% of adults over 70, more commonly in men. Within 12 years of onset, half develop a synucleinopathy, and >90% do so by 25 years. Diagnosis can be supported by detecting alpha-synuclein aggregates. Management includes melatonin (up to 10 mg nightly) or clonazepam (0.5–2.0 mg qhs), though the latter carries risks of falls and confusion.
keyPoints: RBD prevalence: ~2% in adults >70, more common in men
Synucleinopathy risk: 50% within 12 years, >90% by 25 years
Diagnosis: Alpha-synuclein aggregates in spinal fluid or skin biopsy
Management: Melatonin (up to 10 mg nightly), Clonazepam (0.5–2.0 mg qhs) with caution
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PARASOMNIAS (part 2) | CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP DISORDERS (part 1)

items.title: Characteristics and Diagnosis of Delayed Sleep-Wake Phase Disorder (DSWPD)
items.content: DSWPD is marked by delayed sleep onset and wake times, with normal polysomnography results if conducted at the habitual time. About half have an abnormally delayed endogenous circadian phase, assessed by measuring melatonin onset. DLMO in DSWPD occurs later than the typical 8:00–9:00 P.M., aiding diagnosis. Treatment includes phototherapy and melatonin, though relapse is common.
keyPoints: DSWPD features: Delayed sleep/wake times, normal polysomnography at habitual time
Melatonin onset (DLMO): Later than 8:00–9:00 P.M. in DSWPD
Treatment: Phototherapy and melatonin, high relapse rate
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PARASOMNIAS (part 2) | CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP DISORDERS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Advanced Sleep-Wake Phase Disorder (ASWPD)
items.content: ASWPD is characterized by early sleep onset and wake times, often affecting older adults. About 15% cannot stay awake past 5:00 A.M., with twice that number waking too early frequently. Diagnosis involves identifying early dim-light melatonin secretion. Treatment may involve evening phototherapy to delay the circadian phase.
keyPoints: ASWPD features: Early sleep onset/wake times, common in older adults
Melatonin onset: Early in ASWPD patients
Treatment: Evening phototherapy
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · PARASOMNIAS (part 2) | CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP DISORDERS (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Non-24-Hour Sleep-Wake Disorder in Blind Patients
items.content: Non-24-h sleep-wake rhythm disorder (N24SWD) occurs when the circadian pacemaker loses synchronization with the 24-hour day, often due to lack of light perception. This results in intermittent insomnia and excessive daytime sleepiness as the endogenous rhythms drift out of phase with the local environment.
keyPoints: Occurs due to loss of primary synchronizing input (light-dark cycle), especially in blind individuals with no light perception.
Affected patients experience intermittent bouts of insomnia when their circadian rhythms are out of phase with the 24-hour day, and excessive daytime sleepiness.
Symptomatic phases can last several weeks to months depending on the difference between the intrinsic period and the 24-hour day.
Low-dose melatonin (0.5 mg) or a melatonin receptor agonist may improve sleep and induce synchronization.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP DISORDERS (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Shift-Work Disorder on Health and Safety
items.content: Shift-work disorder affects millions, leading to misalignment between circadian rhythms and work-rest schedules. This misalignment causes sleep disturbances, impaired alertness, increased reaction times, and higher risk of accidents.
keyPoints: More than 7 million people in the U.S. regularly work at night or have early morning start times (4:00 A.M. to 7:00 A.M.).
Misalignment between circadian rhythms and sleep-wake cycle increases safety hazards, with sleep disturbance nearly doubling the risk of fatal work accidents.
Sleep deficiency and misaligned circadian phase increase reaction time and performance lapses.
Long-term night-shift workers have higher rates of certain cancers and other health disorders; WHO lists night-shift work as a probable carcinogen.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP DISORDERS (part 2)

items.title: Management Strategies for Circadian Rhythm Sleep Disorders
items.content: Effective management of circadian rhythm sleep disorders involves addressing the underlying causes, such as light exposure and work schedules, and may include pharmacological interventions like melatonin.
keyPoints: In blind patients with N24SWD, low-dose melatonin (0.5 mg) or a melatonin receptor agonist can help synchronize circadian rhythms.
Sighted individuals with N24SWD may experience shifts due to artificial light exposure leading to >24-h schedules.
Shift-work disorder management includes adjusting work-rest schedules and ensuring adequate time off between shifts.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP DISORDERS (part 2)

items.title: Impact of Shift Work on Resident Physicians' Performance
items.content: Prolonged shifts, especially those exceeding 24 consecutive hours, significantly impair psychomotor performance in resident physicians. This impairment is comparable to alcohol intoxication and increases the risk of medical errors.
keyPoints: Shifts ≥24 hours impair psychomotor performance similar to alcohol intoxication.
Double the risk of attentional failures among ICU resident physicians working at night.
Fivefold increase in serious diagnostic mistakes.
20% report fatigue-related patient injury; 5% admit to errors resulting in death.
Increased risk of percutaneous injuries and motor vehicle crashes post-shift.
National Academy of Medicine recommends against scheduling shifts >16 hours without sleep.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP DISORDERS (part 3)

items.title: Characteristics and Risks of Chronic Severe Shift-Work Disorder
items.content: A subset of individuals working night shifts experience significant difficulties staying awake at work and sleeping during the day, leading to chronic severe shift-work disorder. This condition is marked by excessive sleepiness and insomnia with high risks for accidents.
keyPoints: 5–15% of night workers have greater-than-average difficulty remaining awake or sleeping.
Chronic severe SWD involves clinically significant excessive sleepiness at work and insomnia during the day.
Associated with increased risk of sleep-related accidents and illnesses linked to night-shift work.
Patients with chronic severe SWD have a mean sleep latency of 2 minutes during night work, similar to narcolepsy or severe sleep apnea.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · CIRCADIAN RHYTHM SLEEP DISORDERS (part 3)

items.title: Effective Management Strategies for Shift-Work Disorder (SWD)
items.content: Management of SWD involves pharmacological treatments, lifestyle modifications, and organizational strategies. Modafinil or armodafinil can be used to reduce excessive sleepiness during night shifts, though they do not completely eliminate sleepiness. Non-pharmacologic interventions include timed exposure to blue-enriched light, strategic napping, and exercise. Fatigue risk management programs should educate workers about sleep health and screen for sleep disorders.
keyPoints: Modafinil (200 mg) or armodafinil (150 mg) 30–60 min before night shifts reduces excessive sleepiness but does not eliminate it.
Properly timed exposure to blue-enriched light enhances alertness and adaptation to night-shift work.
Fatigue risk management programs should promote education about sleep, increase awareness of hazards, and screen for common sleep disorders.
Work schedules should minimize exposure to night work, frequency of shift rotations, number of consecutive night shifts, and duration of night shifts.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · Shift-Work Disorder

items.title: Designing Work Schedules to Mitigate Shift-Work Disorder Risks
items.content: To reduce the risk of fatigue-related performance lapses in shift workers, work schedules should be carefully designed. This includes minimizing exposure to night shifts, reducing the frequency and number of consecutive night shifts, and shortening the duration of each night shift.
keyPoints: Minimize exposure to night work to reduce fatigue-related risks.
Reduce the frequency of shift rotations to help workers adapt better.
Limit the number of consecutive night shifts to prevent cumulative sleep deprivation.
Shorten the duration of night shifts to decrease overall fatigue.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · Shift-Work Disorder

items.title: Circadian Variation and Cardiovascular Events
items.content: Acute myocardial infarction, sudden cardiac death, and stroke incidence peak in the early morning hours due to increased platelet aggregability. Understanding circadian rhythmicity can aid in grasping the pathophysiology of atherosclerotic disease destabilization.
keyPoints: Increased platelet aggregability occurs in the early morning hours.
Peak incidence of acute myocardial infarction, sudden cardiac death, and stroke aligns with this time.
Circadian rhythmicity may influence acute destabilization of chronic conditions like atherosclerotic disease.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · MEDICAL IMPLICATIONS OF CIRCADIAN RHYTHMICITY

items.title: Impact of Circadian Rhythms on Metabolic and Anesthetic Outcomes
items.content: Chronic circadian disruption and sleep deficiency, as seen in night-shift workers, lead to increased plasma glucose post-meal due to inadequate insulin secretion. Night-shift workers with elevated fasting glucose are at higher risk for diabetes. Blood pressure is higher in night workers with sleep apnea compared to day workers. Drug efficacy and toxicity, including anesthetic agents, vary based on administration time.
keyPoints: Chronic circadian disruption and sleep deficiency increase plasma glucose post-meal due to inadequate insulin secretion.
Night-shift workers with elevated fasting glucose have a higher risk of progressing to diabetes.
Blood pressure is higher in night workers with sleep apnea than day workers.
Drug efficacy and toxicity, including anesthetic agents, are sensitive to time-of-day effects.
items.reference: Sleep Disorders · MEDICAL IMPLICATIONS OF CIRCADIAN RHYTHMICITY
